title: More Sections Make a Better Learning Card

mistaken_claim: If a learning card has more sections, it must be more complete and therefore better.
why_it_seems_plausible: A fuller template looks more rigorous, so people confuse visible completeness with actual retrieval value.
why_it_is_wrong: Extra sections often repeat nearby ideas instead of adding new signal.
More visible scaffolding can hide the actual answer the card is supposed to preserve.
correct_understanding: A better learning card keeps the minimum set of sections that preserve answer quality, graph placement, and next action.
what_it_confuses: It confuses template completion with knowledge compression.
representative_counterexamples: A short card with one clean definition, one boundary rule, and two strong graph links can outperform a bloated card with five weak reflection sections.
trigger_signals: The reviewer hesitates to delete a section only because the template provides it.
Most new content lands in status, checklist, or reflection blocks rather than in the main answer.
corrective_action: Score each section by retrieval value and remove or gate sections that do not change understanding, routing, or action.

common_confusions: [[Complete Template Filling]] | The misconception often appears as loyalty to the template rather than to signal density.
correct_return_nodes: [[Section Priority Compression]] | After correction, return to the trimming method.
adjacent_misconceptions: [[More Detail Means More Understanding]] | Both mistake quantity for learning quality.

prerequisites: [[Single-Card Boundary]] | Without a clear boundary, section growth always feels justified.
enables: [[Section Priority Compression]] | Correcting the belief makes trimming acceptable.
contrasts: [[Signal Density]] | This misconception ignores density and over-values visible fullness.
corrections: [[Section Bloat Drift]] | The mechanism explains how this misconception becomes a recurring template problem.

direct_routes: When the issue is belief-level attachment to template fullness, correct this misconception before trimming anything.
If the user already accepts the correction and needs an execution path, route to [[Section Priority Compression]].
secondary_routes: If trimming still feels unsafe after the misconception is corrected, continue to [[Single-Card Boundary]] to re-anchor what one card is allowed to contain.
gap_signals: If the graph keeps confronting template-overfilling but lacks a card for signal density, add a new Concept card for that criterion.
stop_rules: Stop treating the issue as a misconception once the user accepts the principle and the remaining problem is operational ranking.

current_status_notes: The card already works as a stable local correction node for template-overfilling behavior.
next_goal: Add a stronger priority rule for when this misconception overlaps with scope drift.
growing_checklist: Explain why the misconception looks disciplined rather than sloppy.
stable_checklist: Show reliable return routing after correction.
expert_ready_checklist: Add stronger arbitration when template fullness and topic breadth stack together.

main_reasons: The card already has stable corrective value.
missing_evidence: It still needs stronger arbitration when template fullness and topic breadth are both present.
next_rules: Correct this misconception before trimming only when the real blocker is attachment to fullness rather than lack of boundary clarity.

current_upgrade_tasks: Capture one case where correcting the misconception was not enough because the card was also mis-scoped.
